HAWO was also heard drumming in the background.

Media notes: I thought I was hearing a distant Cooper's Hawk, then as I walked the geometry wasn't right.  Looking around, it was a Blue Jay almost right over my head.  I could see its bill moving as it did the coha calls.  It was maybe 15m up in a completely leafless tree.  There's no mistaking that it was the blja making the sounds.  The typical blja calls at the end are from the same individual, which gives some scaling for the coha mimicry being relatively soft.  Unfortunately, this was all happening right next to a noisy machine.  Cautionary tale- both Merlin and Birdnet returned Cooper's Hawk for this recording.

Long before I joined eBird I had kept 5 recordings from 2023 because the Merlin Sound Id app on my cell phone had identified them as a Townsend’s Solitaire. To my untrained ear, the calls do sound the same as the 1st sample call for this species on All About Birds (and the Merlin app), and the frequency of each call being approximately 2 seconds apart matches this description for the calls of this species as found at AllAboutBirds: "The most commonly heard is a high-pitched clear ringing note that they give about 30 times per minute." After playing these recordings back to more experienced birders they each suggested I post these recordings, so attached is one of them. The other 4 recordings which all sound basically the same with similar frequency in regards to the high-pitched calls. After review (thanks Jeff) it was determined that the calls were of a Blue Jay.

Media notes: The vocal note with the sweep up and drop that you see throughout is a blue jay that was sitting about 5 ft away from me. However, the Merlin app was identifying it as a red tail hawk.

Media notes: 11 BLJA were recorded as they perched in  the trees near a bird feeder area. Unknown short series at 1:53 and 155.8 minutes; perhaps "rattle" call of the BLJA female?
Observation Details: A flock of BLJA flew into the feeder area, joining the few resident BLJA. Some of the BLJA perched in the trees near the feeder area and were very vocal and recorded. Other BLJA were observed foraging on the ground under the feeders. They were present for a brief time period, then left. EVGR were perched in the trees as well. Several short, fast, low pitched (2000 Hz), "ticking" like series were noted through the recording. There did not seem to be an introductory tick as expected for the "rattle" call of the female BLJA on many of the "rattles" on the recording. It was possible that the dark vertical line before the "rattle" at 143.8 and 155.7 may be "ticks" just prior to the BLJA "rattle call". This "rattle-like" call also noted at 4.2, 11.5, 13. 3, 16.5, 34.3 seconds on the recording.

Observation Details: Wow. What an insane series of calls from this Blue Jay. Cellphone audio below. Sorry about the heavy road noise, but, hey, that's authentic. That's birding anytime, anywhere. When I first heard the jay, at some distance, I thought it was going to be one of those fake raptor broadcasts to scare off Canada Geese, etc. (cue Google building in downtown Boulder). But I got closer and realized it was a bird. I was thinking it was going to be a large raptor, but I was also wondering whether it was going to be a Blue Jay, which it was. The bird was holed up in a dense, tall Colorado blue spruce, from where it was giving these whack calls. Listen especially to the end, when the bird goes all out. See also comments under Bushtit.

There was an ongoing jay battle in our yard today the likes of which I have never seen before.  It appeared that only two birds were involved but the fighting was violent. At one point one bird had the other bird upside down on the lawn and was digging its beak into it. I was going to intervene but they flew off on their own. Other birds flew up several times to observe the combatants, but did not interfere. This series of calls was recorded in the first part of the battle. If you listen closely you can hear the birds' wings hitting twigs in the tree they were fighting under.

Observation Details: well darn, it was just a matter of time until the BLJAs at this park learned to imitate the resident RSHAs, some helpful clues of this unwelcome fakery before we got eyeballs on the culprit:  1) calls were faint, like a RSHA sounds a moderate distance away, but triangulation revealed the caller was much closer  2) calls moved around in the treetops instead of coming from a single stationary perch  3) calls alternated with non-alarm BLJA calls and there was only a single bird, not a harassing mob

Observation Details: For a few years there's been a Blue Jay in the vicinity of Sewall Brook where it crosses Forest Street (in between the railroad tracks and the entrance to the Town Forest) that does a very creditable imitation of a Red-tailed Hawk.  (I've actually been able to watch it as it calls.)  I've also wondered whether a Blue Jay might be responsible for isolated calls of a Red-shouldered Hawk that I sometimes hear without being able to locate a hawk to pin them on.  Attached is a recording that includes, in quick succession, the calls (or imitations of the calls) of a Cooper's Hawk, Red-tailed Hawk, and Red-Shouldered Hawk.  The sounds were coming from a tree overlooking Sewall Brook that was very near to me, where I could find neither hide nor hair of any bird of prey.  I believe the sounds were all made by a talented Blue Jay, though I'm sorry to say that I didn't actually _see_ a Blue Jay making them.  There were, however, several Blue Jays in the area (and as I said, no sign of any of the hawks that seemed to be calling; it's very improbable that I failed to catch a glimpse of at least one of the three hawks that seemed to be calling so near to me).  (Added later: I uploaded the file to BirdNET.  It identified the calls as Cooper's Hawk, Red-tailed Hawk, and Red-shouldered Hawk.)

Observation Details: Apparently long continuing but has remained unreported. Finally happened to locate the bird out of pure luck along the river path about 0.25 mile north of south end bathrooms. Came right overhead calling raucously and landed in large elm tree on the river side of the path. Slightly larger than a robin with distinct crest, blue back, wings, and tail, and pale underside. Dark collar stood out against light throat. Very very loud as it scrambled through the branches, constantly gave harsh “Jay- jay-jay” calls which I have a recording of. Only stuck around for 2-3 minutes then took off calling to the north. New patch bird!

Heard in Russian olive thicket while searching for owls and passerines and immediately recognized short, garbling calls as a blue jay. Never did the loud classic blue jay call, only the watery and mechanical quieter calls (attached). First detected and eventually (poorly) photographed here: 42.899994, -115.861469. Followed the individual through the thicket, recording all the way, from the dirt road through the thicket to the water and back. Was vaguely associating with a group of robins. Skulky, quiet for a blue jay, and often staying fairly low in thicket pecking against branches. Photo and audio

The homeowner requests that anyone who wants to try for the bird plans to park at the junction of Table Bluff Road and Hookton Cemetery Road (40.659043, -124.216363), or just below there at the Hookton Cemetery (40.660674, -124.214201).  The homeowner further requests that people only come on the property between 2:00 and 5:00 p.m., which is the approximate period of time when the bird was seen today.   Sean has volunteered to liaison, so please direct any acccess questions to him via email: whiteouters@gmail.com

Observation Details: As soon as we had gotten out of the truck looking for the swamp sparrow, we had started walking through the gate that led to the ponds south of the parking lot, we heard a strange, raspy jay like call. I was immediately intrigued by this call, knowing it unusual, thinking it was some sort of jay. I started walking towards it, where it was coming from a grove of trees that acted as a wall to the north of the parking lot. As I reached the first set of trees before said wall, I attempted recordings with Merlin, which didn't identify the call. When I was getting ready to move past the trees, a flicker flew into some trees from where the jay was calling, with me thinking it was him, I bolted out to get my camera on the flicker, only to discover that it was not the jay, who stopped calling.   Disappointed, I started walking back, when the jay started calling again, this time I got it confirmed on Merlin. After trying to get closer again and the jay going quiet again, we backed out again and the jay called, this time becoming visible at the tops of the trees. He then flew to our right to some other trees, where we got some poor quality pictures of him. As I looked down at my camera looking at the pictures, he had since flown again and was not heard again.  The call that we heard which I got recorded was a raspy *jay jay* call that was unmistakable, I got three or four different recordings of him doing that call, as well as a set of poor quality pictures of him at the top of the trees.

Observation Details: Interesting behavior. An adult red-tailed hawk had captured and killed a jay. As it was plucking and eating it beneath a tree, a dozen jays noisily descended on the hawk to exact revenge for their now-deceased comrade. The hawk, finally dealt all it could bear, flew off to eat its meal in the canopy of a pine. The jays stayed and held a service for their dearly departed. They called loudly, and one jay flew down to the ground, picked up a feather from the dead bird, and finding no life beneath it, dropped it, and retreated into the trees, where it joined the others in calling loudly for 5 minutes. Blue Jays and Scrub Jays are known to hold "funerals," so it was cool to see one in real life. The audio is from after the hawk left.

Observation Details: Recordings to be uploaded (which have been edited in Voice Record Pro to increase volume and decrease wind noise), no photos. First heard calls that I immediately thought were Blue Jay, coming from this area (32.245968, -106.818580) while I was on the upland trail. Very familiar with these calls having lived in locations where Blue Jays are abundant. Made my way towards that area and ~10 minutes later heard calling again from here (32.245867, -106.816119). As I was walking into the river bottom to try to find the bird, I got a quick glimpse of a bird flushing from the sandbar where I thought the bird was calling from towards the pecans to the east. The quick view I got was of a bird similar in size and shape to a scrub-jay, with a relatively long tail, backlit so I could not discern color. Heard calling one more time from the pecans to the east.   Mockingbirds were in the area, but the calling was not repeated as a mockingbird would and only Blue Jay calls were given (including 2 different vocalization types). The other possibility would be playbacks in the pecan grove but I am confident that these calls were from a bird given the movement in area that I was hearing the calls and the variability in the calls and when calls were occurring. Any other thoughts/comments on possibilities other than Blue Jay would be appreciated.

I should note that much later in the day I did see a hawk (but I usually only see cooper's close to homes). Media notes: Two Blue Jays, one doing Red-shouldered Hawk mimicry.
Observation Details: One bird doing Red-shouldered  Hawk, Broad-winged Hawk (not picked up on recording), Starling & American Goldfinch imitations on the ground below the feeder, mixed with normal Blue Jay calls (an actual American Goldfinch is also present in the first recording).  These imitations are common occurrences, especially the BWHA mimicry, which is heard almost every day.  This bird appeared almost bored, and was hopping around acting playfully aggressive toward squirrels and other birds around the feeder station.  It had plenty to eat beforehand and did not seem food-motivated.  Note to anyone birding at this location: if you hear what sounds like a Broad-winged or Red-shouldered Hawk's but don't see one, it's far more likely to be a Blue Jay!  These 2 imitations consistently fool Merlin as well.  Other past imitations include Cooper's Hawk, Bald Eagle & Osprey.

First heard the stressed and charismatic alarm calls of several and I thought “there must be an owl or something back there,” so I ran inside, threw on some pants, grabbed a tripod and tip-toed to the scene. When I arrived, I heard the screaming of jays, red-tails, some nuthatch calling and wrens singing, as well as a brief note from the owl. I then saw a little deformity in the tree that all the jays were congregated on, zoomed in, and saw the Owl!!!

fairly amazing mimicry experience from what is surely the same resident Blue Jay that has been singing and occasionally mimicing American Crow in the yard for the past two years. In this case, Ithrew out some birdseed on the deck and this bird flew in and started giving the AMCR immediately. It continued for several minutes and eventually switched to normal Blue Jay calls after dropping down to the deck nervously just a few feet away from me and immediately flying back up. The whole time the bird was within 20 ft of me and changed perched several times. The two recording are the same: one the long version and the other a boosted version of the mimicry.

Observation Details: I had just arrived home and was getting out of my car when I heard this bird call, which I do not recall hearing before. It sounds like the chain on a swing set swinging back and forth. I looked around and up in my oak trees and was surprised to see a Blue Jay high in the tree. I have observed Blue Jays many times and thought I was familiar with all of thier calls and songs. I had not heard this one before. The Blue Jay kept this up for several minutes and other than that it was very quiet with I believe another Blue Jay responding from a distance but no other birds appearing.. Finally, another Blue Jay flew over. At that time, the singing Blue Jay stopped singing and dropped down to mid level in the tree and stared at me for a minute before flying over my house.

Observation Details: Interesting interaction: the four birds flew together into the top of a cottonwood. They perched together and began a sort of call and response behavior. One bird bobbed while emitting a woody sounding single-note trill. Another responded with a rattle while bobbing more rapidly. These two birds were facing one another as they exchanged these calls.  Then a third bird made the flute like two note call but not as frequently as the two who were “conversing” and this bird was facing away from the two. The four stayed together during the exchange, which lasted about a minute before flying off together. Faint recording will be posted soon

Media notes: Sorry about the motorcycle parade down on the highway.  More than a mile away, but the wind was blowing from that direction. Bird was perched in a tree and calling several times. I only captured one of those calls. He then flew past me - which is when I called to him ( imaginatively I named him BJ last fall when he first appeared and that has morphed into  "Beej")- and landed in a lilac clump. That is where he began calling again. Until he found  a buried peanut. Then he grabbed his stash and flew back to the pine tree.
Observation Details: Back after an absence of about two weeks. He flew in and landed in his favorite pine tree just north of the house. After doing the Tweedledee call he flew across the deck and landed in a lilac grove where he did the loud scratchy call. He continued to do that call until he jumped down into the grass and found a peanut. He took the peanut and flew back over toward his favorite pine tree. Later, after the count, he came to the deck for a peanut and a drink from the bird bath.

Media notes: Red-shouldered Hawk mimicry, with mimicry of another species (likely European Starling) preceding & following
Observation Details: Doing loud  Red-shouldered Hawk mimicry in tree above feeder, with some other call imitation (possibly European Starling) interjected several times.  Photographed while vocalizing.  This behavior has been heard and observed numerous times in the yard near the feeder station, with RSHA imitations the most heard, but also clear examples of Broad-winged and occasional Cooper's, sometimes in the same call sequences.  Its purpose generally seems to be to rattle or distract the feeder birds & squirrels, although it never seems entirely effective at driving them off.  However it does tend to irritate other species from chickadees to crows, as heard in the audio.  I'm uncertain if it's just one talented Jay that's been doing all the mimicry over the past year or so, but I've never heard more than one doing imitations at the same time.  OTHER RECORDINGS of Blue Jay hawk mimicry at the same spot: https://ebird.org/atlasme/checklist/S83702848  (3-19-21): https://ebird.org/checklist/S69850124 (5-28-20)
Hope to be a mating pair.

Media notes: This is not a continuous recording, it is three separate cuts focussed on some low, flat buzzy rattle calls being made by a Blue Jay. Cuts were taken from a longer recording, with 3 seconds of silence inserted between each cut segment. Took a lot of sleuth work to figure out what the low, flat, buzzy calls were. I suspected a Blue Jay, but didn't think I could confirm it until I found a recording on xeno-canto that had an identical sound that had been confirmed as a Blue Jay rattle call. Assumed to be an adult Blue Jay, given the time of year. The confirming recording on xeno-canto can be found at https://www.xeno-canto.org/511960
Observation Details: Conservative count. Numerous in the east bush. Blue Jays on the other hand are abundant in the garden, loud and opinionated.  So, I am  thinking this must be a Blue Jay.   This teaches me to be very careful with ID's from BirdNET.
